definition: sql used to define tables, columns, constraints, and relationships (e.g., create table, alter table).
field that links to a row in another table to enforce referential integrity.
unique, not null identifier for each row in a table.
128-bit globally unique identifier, suitable for distributed systems.
diagram that models entities (tables) and relationships for schema design.
relationship where rows in each table can relate to many rows in the other, implemented via a join table.
relationship where one row in a parent table links to many rows in a child table.
plain-text tabular format commonly used to import or export data.
binary json type supporting indexing and rich operators.
user-defined type whose values are a fixed set of labels.
information that can identify an individual and requires stricter controls.
programmatic interface for accessing database-backed services.
four fundamental data operations a system must support: create, read, update, delete.
transaction guarantees that ensure reliable, recoverable changes (atomicity, consistency, isolation, durability).
atomic unit of work that begins with begin and ends with commit or rollback.
settings (read committed, repeatable read, serializable) that control what concurrent transactions can see.
multi-version concurrency control using row versions so readers don’t block writers and vice versa.
write-ahead logging that records changes before writing data pages, enabling crash recovery.
available index methods (b-tree, hash, gist, sp-gist, gin, brin) optimised for different query patterns.
technique that splits a large table into partitions for performance and manageability.
physical or logical copying of wal or row changes from a primary to standbys or subscribers.
workloads optimised for many small reads and writes with low latency.
analytical workloads over large, often historical, datasets.
command that shows how the planner will execute a query; analyse adds timings.
stored result of a query that can be refreshed for faster reads.
guarantee that child rows reference valid parent rows via foreign keys.
schema design discipline that reduces redundancy and anomalies using normal forms.
principle stating that under partition a distributed system cannot provide both strong consistency and availability.
guarantees such as strong, eventual, and causal that describe read/write behaviour in distributed databases.
horizontal partitioning that distributes data across nodes for scale-out.
source: ACM Computing Surveys — Large-Scale Data Management

definition: concurrency primitives (table, row, advisory) used to coordinate access.
declarative integrity rules such as not null, unique, check, exclude, foreign key, and primary key.
unique, not null identifier for each row, usually backed by an index.
wal durability milestone that flushes dirty buffers and records a checkpoint to bound recovery time.
point-in-time recovery that restores a cluster to a chosen time using base backups and archived wal.
analyse-collected histograms and most-common values used by the cost-based optimiser.
source: PostgreSQL Documentation — Statistics Used by the Planner

definition: maintenance that reclaims dead tuples and prevents transaction id wraparound.
users and groups that own objects and hold privileges for authentication and authorisation.
permissions that control who can connect, use schemas, and read or write objects.
policies that restrict which rows a role may select, insert, update, or delete.
object that yields unique numeric values, often used for surrogate keys.
standard auto-increment column generated by an implicit sequence (generated as identity).
constrained base type that encapsulates validation rules for reuse.
column type that stores arrays with dedicated operators and functions.
interval value types (e.g., tsrange, daterange) with overlap and containment semantics; multiranges hold disjoint ranges.
column whose value is computed from an expression and may be stored or virtual.
virtual table defined by a named query and not physically stored.
namespaces that organise objects; search_path sets name-resolution order.
index built only on rows matching a predicate to reduce size and maintenance.
index on the result of an expression to speed repeated calculations or filters.
